source_metadata.title_raw: David E. Kendig - A-18-123 - July 23, 2018 - Costa Mesa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) ;322 - 5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
David E. Kendig 
General Counsel 
Woodruff, Spradlin & ;Smart 
555 Anton Blvd., Suite 1200 
Costa Mesa, CA 92626-7670 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice. 
Our File No. A-18-123 
Dear Mr. Kendig: 
July 20, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act") 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing 
advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section I 090, not under other general 
conflict of interest prohibitions outside the purview of the Act. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 197 5) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. lfthis is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
I. May the Orange County Fire Authority ("OCF A") Fire Chief Brian Fennessy participate 
in discussions regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management if the discussions may 
result in triggering a clause in his existing employment agreement that affects his annual 
compensation? 
2. May the OCF A Human Resources Director ("HR Dii;ector") participate in discussions 
regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management where the discussions may result in 
elevating that position to an Assistant Chief level position? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 l000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-123 
Page No. 2 
3. May Fire Chief Fennessy, the HR Director and Assistant Chief in charge of Business 
Services participate in discussions regarding negotiating salaries and benefits with two labor group 
if those three positions stand to indirectly benefit from any salary or benefit increases authorized for 
the labor groups? 
4. Where Fire Chief Fennessy's employment agreement entitles him to the benefits afforded 
to all other members of Executive Management by the Authority's Personnel and Salary Resolution 
("P.SR"), would amendments to the PSR to change those benefits for all Executive Management 
employees be considered a "contract made by them" for purposes of Section 1090 such that the Fire 
Chief would be prohibited from participating in any discussions about changes to the PSR? 
CONCLUSIONS 
l. No. As explained below, Fire Chief Fennessy may not participate in discussions regarding 
a possible restructuring of the OCF A management staff. 
2. No. As explained below, the OCF A HR Director may not participate in discussions 
regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management staff where the discussions may result 
in elevating that position to an Assistant Chief level position? 
3. Yes. As explained below, Fire Chief Fennessy, the HR Director and Assistant Chief in 
charge of Business Services may participate in discussions regarding negotiating salaries and 
benefits with the two labor organizations. 
4. No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit Fire Chief Fennessy from 
participating in decisions to modify the PSR. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
Fire Chief Fennessy's Emplo ment Contract 
You serve as General Counsel to the Orange County Fire Authority ("OCF A"), a Joint 
Powers Authority headed by Fire Chief Brian Fennessy. His employment agreement with the 
OCFA contains a provision at Section 5.C., titled "Salary," which provides that "(a]fter the first 
year, future annual non-base building increases will be awarded automatically to ensure that the 
Fire Chiefs salary when combined with this increase will equal five percent (5%) above the salary 
of Fire Chiefs highest paid subordinate, excluding overtime." This means his salary will be 
automatically increased each year, when necessary, to ensure that his salary is at least 5% greater 
than his highest paid subordinate, excluding overtime. 
The Human Resources Director and Assistant Chief in Charge of Business Services 
The HR Director and the Assistant Chief in Charge of Business Services are positions at 
OCF A that do not have employment agreements. Neither position is represented by a labor 
organization, so there is no MOU or other contract that establishes their salary and benefits. Instead, 
the salaries for these two positions are set forth in a Salary Table and the benefits for the two 


File No. A-18-123 
Page No. 3 
positions are set forth in the PSR, which is a resolution adopted unilaterally by the Board of 
Directors of the OCFA. It does not involve or require an offer and acceptance like other l:Ontracts. 
Likewise, the Master Position Control and Salary Table are documents approved and 
am~nded by unilateral action of the Board of Directors. Although the effects of amending a PSR oi 
Master Position Control document can be subject to negotiation with represented and unrepresented 
groups of employees, the Board nevertheless retains the ultimate authority to impose amendments 
to the PSR or Master Position Control and Salary Table, whether or not an agreement results from 
negotiation~. The HR Director and the Assistant Chief in charge of the Business Services 
Department are often consulted by the Board on matters that are subject to labor negotiations. 
Potential Reinstatement of a Deputy Chief Position 
The position of Fire Chief is at the fop of the OCFA management structure (subordinate 
only to the Board of Directors). The tier below the Fire Chief are managers at the Assistant Chief 
level. The OCF A may soon consider structural changes to OCF A management, including whether 
to reinstate one or more Deputy Chief positions between the Fire Chief and the Assistant Chief tiers 
in the management structure. It is likely the Fire Chief will want to comment upon, and the Board 
will want to hear his views on, the desirability of those management changes. 
The Fire Chiefs compensation is currently 5% greater than that of the Assistant Chief, his 
highest paid subordinate. If the Board elects to reinstate one or more Deputy Chief positions 
between the Fire Chief and the Assistant Chief, and to include with that position a salary higher 
than the Assistant Chief, the Fire Chief's salary would automatically increase to be 5% greater than 
the·reinstated Deputy C~ief(s) pursuant to his employment agreement. 
Other possible managerial changes that might be proposed for the Board's consideration 
during a reorganization discussion include amending the OCF A's Master Position Control by 
elevating the HR Director position to an Assistant Chief level position, with a corresponding 
increase in that position's compensation. 
ANALYSIS 
1, 2 and 3. Restructuring of the OCFA Management Staff and Negotiating Salaries and 
Benefits 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of 
the public official's interests. (Section 87103.) 
In addition to the other interests set forth in Section 87103, a public official always has an 
int€rest in his or her personal finances. The management decisions pertaining to restructuring 
management and salaries/benefits would affect the personal finances of Fire Chief Fennessy, the 
HR Director and the Assistant Chief in charge of Business Services. 


For.eseeability and Materiality 
File No. A-18-123 
Page No. 4 
The standard for determining foreseeability is set forth in Regulation 18701, and depends 
upon whether the financial interest is "explicitly involved" in the government decision. Where, as 
here, the personal finances of the officials are explicitly involved in the decisions in question, the 
effect is deemed reasonably foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.S(a) provides that "[a] personal financial effect means the financial effect 
of a governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate 
family. The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will 
receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision."2 
The potential increased salary and benefits for the three officials would constitute a 
measurable financial effect on their personal finances. Thus, absent an exception, the officials 
would haye a conflict of interest. Since they would have a conflict of interest, Regulation 18704 
specifies that they are prohibited from doing any of the following: 
(a) Making a Decision. A public official makes a governmental decision 
if the official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, 
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters 
into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a 
governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or 
a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without 
significant intervening substantive review. 
(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public 
official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision if he or she: 
( 1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an 
agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency 
for the purpose of affecting a decision; or 
(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government 
agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official 
acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or 
her agency in making the contact. 
But Regulation 18704(d)(3) provides an exc~ption stating that a public official does not 
make, participate in making or influence a governmental decision in situations that involve: 
2 Note in January 2015, the Commission removed the $250 and replaced it with the current standard of 
"measurable financial benefit or loss" which is determined on a case-by-case basis. 


File No. A-18-123 
Page No. 5 
Actions by a public official relating to his or her compensation or 
the terms or conditions of his or her employment or consulting contract. 
However, an official may not make a decision to appoint, hire, fire, 
promote, demote, or suspend without pay or take disciplinary action with 
financial sanction against the official or his or her immediate family, or 
set a salary for the official or his or her immediate family different from 
salaries paid to other employees of the government agency in the same 
job classification or position. 
Fire Chief Fennessy 
You state that the OCF A Board will consider reinstating one or more Deputy Chiefs, a new 
level of management that would fall between the current Fire Chief and Assistant Chieflevels. We 
view this decision as a promotion for Fire Chief Fennessy as it adds another layer of management 
under his supervision and control, while _simultaneously providing an automatic increase in his 
salary - a contractually guaranteed increase that will amount to 5% more than the salary of the new 
Deputy Chief(s) because that position will be compensated at a higher salary than the current 
Assistant Chief. 
Accordingly, Fire Chief Fennessy may not.make, participate in making, or use his official 
position to influence a decision concerning potential reinstatement of Deputy Chiefs to the current 
OCF A management structure. 
Human Resources Director 
You state that the OCF A Board may consider other managerial changes that include 
elevating the current HR Director position to the Assistant Chief level, which will result in a 
corresponding increase in the HR Director's compensation. This move would effectively serve as a 
promotion for that position. 
Accordingly, the current HR Director may not make, participate in making, or use his or her 
official position to influence a decision concerning the elevation of that position to the Assistant 
Chief level. 
Salary and Benefits Negotiations 
You inquire whether Fire Chief Fennessy, the HR Director and Assistant Chief in charge of 
Business Services may participate in discussions regarding negotiating salaries and benefits with 
the two labor organizations if those three positions stand to benefit from any salary or benefit 
increases authorized for these two labor groups. So long as they are not setting a salary for 
themselves that is different from salaries paid to other employees in the same job classification or 
.position, they may participate in such discussions. 3 
3 This exception also covers employment-related benefits, such as pensions. (In re Moore.(1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 
33, Estrada Advice Letter, No. 1-15-232.) 


4. Section 1090 - Amendments to the PSR 
File No. A-18-123 
Page No. 6 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety. ( City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
The determinative issue here is whether the PSR, a resolution setting forth benefits for 
OCF A management staff, is a contract for purposes of Section 1090. To determine whether a 
contract is involved in a decision, the Section I 090 analysis looks to general principles of contract 
law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230,234 (1995)), while keeping in 
mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require that we view the transactions 
in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 'contract."' (People v. Honig, 
(1996) 48 Cal. App. 4th 289, 351 citing Stigall v: City ofTqft (1962) 58 Cal.2d at 569, 571, See also 
Wilson Advice Letter No. A-16-269). In this regard, "[a) contract is an agreement to do or not to do 
a certain thing." (Civ. Code, § 1549 .) "It is essential to a contract that there should be: 1. Parties 
capable of contracting; 2. Their consent; 3. A lawful object; and, 4. A sufficient cause or 
consideration." (Civ. Code,§ 1550.)4 
Mindful that we should avoid narrow and technical definitions of "contract" in the present 
situation, it is nonetheless essential to every contract, as just mentioned, that there be parties 
capable of contracting as well as consent of the parties. Here, there are no contracting parties to the 
PSA and no mutual consent of those contracting parties. Instead, the PSA is a resolution adopted 
unilaterally by the OCF A Board and it does not involve or require an offer and acceptance like 
other contracts. Indeed, the Board retains the ultimate authority to impose amendments to the PSR. 
Thus, the essential features present in every contract simply are not present in the current situation. 
Therefore, we cannot conclude, without those essential elements present, that the PSA is a contract 
for purposes of Section 1090. 
Accordingly, because the PSR is not a contract for purposes of Section 1090, Fire Chief 
Fennessy may participate in decisions to modify the PSR.5 
4 The Attorney General cited to these contracting principles when determining whether a development 
agreement constituted a contract for purposes of Section 1090. (See 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 233 (1995).) 
5 Similarly, taking part in decisions regarding the PSR is not prohibited under the Act pursuant to Regulation 
18704( d)(3) because the action relates to the officials' compensation or the terms or conditions of their employment 
contract and would not be setting a salary for their position different from other officials in the same job classification 
or position. 


File No. A-18-123 
Page No: 7, 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
BrianG. Lau 
Acting General Counsel · 
C 0~ 
Se ior Counsel, Legal Division

highest paid subordinate. If the Board elects to reinstate one or more Deputy Chief positions 
between the Fire Chief and the Assistant Chief, and to include with that position a salary higher

the·reinstated Deputy C~ief(s) pursuant to his employment agreement. 
Other possible managerial changes that might be proposed for the Board's consideration 
during a reorganization discussion include amending the OCF A's Master Position Control by 
elevating the HR Director position to an Assistant Chief level position, with a corresponding

sections.analysis:
1, 2 and 3. Restructuring of the OCFA Management Staff and Negotiating Salaries and 
Benefits 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of

In addition to the other interests set forth in Section 87103, a public official always has an 
int€rest in his or her personal finances. The management decisions pertaining to restructuring 
management and salaries/benefits would affect the personal finances of Fire Chief Fennessy, the 
HR Director and the Assistant Chief in charge of Business Services.

The standard for determining foreseeability is set forth in Regulation 18701, and depends

of a governmental decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate

if the official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a person, 
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters 
into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a 
governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or 
a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without 
significant intervening substantive review. 
(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A public 
official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision if he or she: 
( 1) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an 
agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency 
for the purpose of affecting a decision; or 
(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government 
agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official 
acts or purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or 
her agency in making the contact. 
But Regulation 18704(d)(3) provides an exc~ption stating that a public official does not 
make, participate in making or influence a governmental decision in situations that involve: 
2 Note in January 2015, the Commission removed the $250 and replaced it with the current standard of 
"measurable financial benefit or loss" which is determined on a case-by-case basis.  
Actions by a public official relating to his or her compensation or 
the terms or conditions of his or her employment or consulting contract. 
However, an official may not make a decision to appoint, hire, fire, 
promote, demote, or suspend without pay or take disciplinary action with 
financial sanction against the official or his or her immediate family, or 
set a salary for the official or his or her immediate family different from 
salaries paid to other employees of the government agency in the same 
job classification or position.

You state that the OCF A Board will consider reinstating one or more Deputy Chiefs, a new 
level of management that would fall between the current Fire Chief and Assistant Chieflevels. We 
view this decision as a promotion for Fire Chief Fennessy as it adds another layer of management 
under his supervision and control, while _simultaneously providing an automatic increase in his 
salary - a contractually guaranteed increase that will amount to 5% more than the salary of the new

Assistant Chief. 
Accordingly, Fire Chief Fennessy may not.make, participate in making, or use his official 
position to influence a decision concerning potential reinstatement of Deputy Chiefs to the current 
OCF A management structure. 
Human Resources Director 
You state that the OCF A Board may consider other managerial changes that include 
elevating the current HR Director position to the Assistant Chief level, which will result in a 
corresponding increase in the HR Director's compensation. This move would effectively serve as a 
promotion for that position. 
Accordingly, the current HR Director may not make, participate in making, or use his or her 
official position to influence a decision concerning the elevation of that position to the Assistant 
Chief level.

You inquire whether Fire Chief Fennessy, the HR Director and Assistant Chief in charge of 
Business Services may participate in discussions regarding negotiating salaries and benefits with 
the two labor organizations if those three positions stand to benefit from any salary or benefit 
increases authorized for these two labor groups. So long as they are not setting a salary for 
themselves that is different from salaries paid to other employees in the same job classification or 
.position, they may participate in such discussions. 3 
3 This exception also covers employment-related benefits, such as pensions. (In re Moore.(1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 
33, Estrada Advice Letter, No. 1-15-232.) 


4. Section 1090 - Amendments to the PSR

Fennessy may participate in decisions to modify the PSR.5 
4 The Attorney General cited to these contracting principles when determining whether a development 
agreement constituted a contract for purposes of Section 1090. (See 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 233 (1995).) 
5 Similarly, taking part in decisions regarding the PSR is not prohibited under the Act pursuant to Regulation 
18704( d)(3) because the action relates to the officials' compensation or the terms or conditions of their employment 
contract and would not be setting a salary for their position different from other officials in the same job classification 
or position.

in discussions regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management if the discussions may 
result in triggering a clause in his existing employment agreement that affects his annual 
compensation? 
2. May the OCF A Human Resources Director ("HR Dii;ector") participate in discussions 
regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management where the discussions may result in 
elevating that position to an Assistant Chief level position? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 l000 through 91014. 3. May Fire Chief Fennessy, the HR Director and Assistant Chief in charge of Business 
Services participate in discussions regarding negotiating salaries and benefits with two labor group 
if those three positions stand to indirectly benefit from any salary or benefit increases authorized for 
the labor groups? 
4. Where Fire Chief Fennessy's employment agreement entitles him to the benefits afforded 
to all other members of Executive Management by the Authority's Personnel and Salary Resolution 
("P.SR"), would amendments to the PSR to change those benefits for all Executive Management 
employees be considered a "contract made by them" for purposes of Section 1090 such that the Fire 
Chief would be prohibited from participating in any discussions about changes to the PSR?

CONCLUSION: l. No. As explained below, Fire Chief Fennessy may not participate in discussions regarding 
a possible restructuring of the OCF A management staff. 
2. No. As explained below, the OCF A HR Director may not participate in discussions 
regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management staff where the discussions may result 
in elevating that position to an Assistant Chief level position? 
3. Yes. As explained below, Fire Chief Fennessy, the HR Director and Assistant Chief in 
charge of Business Services may participate in discussions regarding negotiating salaries and 
benefits with the two labor organizations. 
4. No. As explained below, Section 1090 does not prohibit Fire Chief Fennessy from 
participating in decisions to modify the PSR. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
Fire Chief Fennessy's Emplo ment Contract 
You serve as General Counsel to the Orange County Fire Authority ("OCF A"), a Joint 
Powers Authority headed by Fire Chief Brian Fennessy. His employment agreement with the 
OCFA contains a provision at Section 5.C., titled "Salary," which provides that "(a]fter the first 
year, future annual non-base building increases will be awarded automatically to ensure that the 
Fire Chiefs salary when combined with this increase will equal five percent (5%) above the salary 
of Fire Chiefs highest paid subordinate, excluding overtime." This means his salary will be 
automatically increased each year, when necessary, to ensure that his salary is at least 5% greater 
than his highest paid subordinate, excluding overtime. 
The Human Resources Director and Assistant Chief in Charge of Business Services 
The HR Director and the Assistant Chief in Charge of Business Services are positions at 
OCF A that do not have employment agreements. Neither position is represented by a labor 
organization, so there is no MOU or other contract that establishes their salary and benefits. Instead, 
the salaries for these two positions are set forth in a Salary Table and the benefits for the two  
positions are set forth in the PSR, which is a resolution adopted unilaterally by the Board of 
Directors of the OCFA. It does not involve or require an offer and acceptance like other l:Ontracts. 
Likewise, the Master Position Control and Salary Table are documents approved and 
am~nded by unilateral action of the Board of Directors. Although the effects of amending a PSR oi 
Master Position Control document can be subject to negotiation with represented and unrepresented 
groups of employees, the Board nevertheless retains the ultimate authority to impose amendments 
to the PSR or Master Position Control and Salary Table, whether or not an agreement results from 
negotiation~. The HR Director and the Assistant Chief in charge of the Business Services 
Department are often consulted by the Board on matters that are subject to labor negotiations. 
Potential Reinstatement of a Deputy Chief Position 
The position of Fire Chief is at the fop of the OCFA management structure (subordinate 
only to the Board of Directors). The tier below the Fire Chief are managers at the Assistant Chief 
level. The OCF A may soon consider structural changes to OCF A management, including whether 
to reinstate one or more Deputy Chief positions between the Fire Chief and the Assistant Chief tiers 
in the management structure. It is likely the Fire Chief will want to comment upon, and the Board 
will want to hear his views on, the desirability of those management changes. 
The Fire Chiefs compensation is currently 5% greater than that of the Assistant Chief, his 
highest paid subordinate. If the Board elects to reinstate one or more Deputy Chief positions 
between the Fire Chief and the Assistant Chief, and to include with that position a salary higher 
than the Assistant Chief, the Fire Chief's salary would automatically increase to be 5% greater than 
the·reinstated Deputy C~ief(s) pursuant to his employment agreement. 
Other possible managerial changes that might be proposed for the Board's consideration 
during a reorganization discussion include amending the OCF A's Master Position Control by 
elevating the HR Director position to an Assistant Chief level position, with a corresponding 
increase in that position's compensation.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) ;322 - 5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 David E. Kendig General Counsel Woodruff, Spradlin & ;Smart 555 Anton Blvd., Suite 1200 Costa Mesa, CA 92626-7670 Re: Your Request for Advice. Our File No. A-18-123 Dear Mr. Kendig: July 20, 2018 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") 1 and Section 1090. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section I 090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions outside the purview of the Act. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby ( 197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. lfthis is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) QUESTIONS I. May the Orange County Fire Authority ("OCF A") Fire Chief Brian Fennessy participate in discussions regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management if the discussions may result in triggering a clause in his existing employment agreement that affects his annual compensation? 2. May the OCF A Human Resources Director ("HR Dii;ector") participate in discussions regarding a possible restructuring of the OCF A management where the discussions may result in elevating that position to an Assistant Chief level position? 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 l000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-18-123 Page No. 2 3. May Fire Chief Fennessy, the HR Director and Assistant Chief in charge of Business Services participate in discussions regarding negotiating salaries and benefits with two labor group if those three positions stand to indirectly benefit from any salary or benefit increases authorized for the labor groups? 4. Where Fire Chief Fennessy's employment agreement entitles him to the benefits afforded to all other members of Executive Management by the Authority's Personnel and Salary Resolution ("P.SR"), would amendments to the PSR to change those benefits for
